coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of ionic liquid-functionalized carbon dots as lubricant additive for friction and wear reduction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Shenghua
Zhang, Xiaozhi
Wang, Yi
Jin, Guangkai
Liu, Shujuan
Ye, Qian
Liu, Weimin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a long-chain ionic liquid with the catecholic group was successfully synthesized and then grafted to the carbon dots surface (CDs-ILs-Br) via the self-assembly capability of catecholic compound. The resulting CDs-ILs-Br exhibit excellent dispersion stability in PEG200, maintaining a uniform dispersion for over one month. With the addition of 3.0 wt% CDs-ILs-Br, the average COF of base oil decreased to 0.113, resulting in 60 % reduction in wear volume. Furthermore, the introduction of anti-wear agent BF4 - further reduced average COF to 0.093. XPS and EDS analysis confirm complex tribochemical reactions between CDs-ILs-Br and the friction pair, and the grafting polar molecules of ILs on the carbon dots surface enhanced adhesion effect under high shear condition, forming a robust tribochemical protective film.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dispersion stability
Ionic liquid
Lubricant additive

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The preparation process of CDs-ILs-Br, (b) TG curves, (c) N 1 s and (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-ILs-Br. (e) TEM images of CDs-ILs-Br (inset: HRTEM images).
The photographs and particle size distribution of CDs-ILs-Br.
(a) COF curves, (b) wear volume and average COF, (c) cross-sectional profiles, (d) SEM images of different wear scar (scale bar: 10 µm).
The Br 3d and F 1 s peaks of samples before and after ion exchange.
(a) COF curves of CDs-ILs-BF4 and CDs-ILs-Br (inset: SEM image of wear scar); (b) EDS images of wear scar lubricated by CDs-ILs-BF4 (scale bar: 10 µm).
XPS spectrum of the wear scar based Dop-ILs-BF4: (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, (c)F 1 s, (d) Cu LMM.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of ionic liquid-functionalized carbon dots as lubricant additive for friction and wear reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an, 710072, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a long-chain ionic liquid with the catecholic group was successfully synthesized and then grafted to the carbon dots surface (CDs-ILs-Br) via the self-assembly capability of catecholic compound. The resulting CDs-ILs-Br exhibit excellent dispersion stability in PEG200, maintaining a uniform dispersion for over one month. With the addition of 3.0 wt% CDs-ILs-Br, the average COF of base oil decreased to 0.113, resulting in 60 % reduction in wear volume. Furthermore, the introduction of anti-wear agent BF4 - further reduced average COF to 0.093. XPS and EDS analysis confirm complex tribochemical reactions between CDs-ILs-Br and the friction pair, and the grafting polar molecules of ILs on the carbon dots surface enhanced adhesion effect under high shear condition, forming a robust tribochemical protective film.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear are unavoidable problems in industrial production, resulting in an annual global economic loss equivalent to 2 % of the GDP [1]. Lubrication is a primary approach to reducing friction, while base oil is the most commonly used liquid lubricant in various production activities. As human production activities advance and extreme working conditions become more common, there is a growing demand for advanced lubricant additives that offer high quality and long-lasting lubrication performance [2,3]. Nanomaterials including C3N4 [4], MXene [5,6], carbon nanospheres [7], hexagonal BN [8], and MoS2 [9], have gradually gained attention due to their straightforward synthesis, ease of modification, and unique physical and chemical properties [10,11]. However, the high surface energy of nanoparticles makes them prone to agglomeration over time, which can significantly impair lubrication performance of base oil [12–14]. Therefore, developing nano-additives with long-term stability is crucial to addressing this issue.

Carbon dots (CDs), first reported in 2004, are a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with the diameter less than 10 nm [15]. Their availability form diverse raw material and low preparation cost render CDs a sort of highly promising nanomaterials [16–18]. Simultaneously, CDs possess excellent water solubility, fluorescence, chemical stability, and environmental friendliness, making them suitable for application in catalysis [19], fluorescence probe [20], cell staining [21] and other fields. In lubrication applications, the small size of CDs allows them to effectively repair the friction pairs, establishing them as nano-additives with significant potential [22,23]. For example, Lu et al. [24] synthesized CDs using a hydrothermal method and then coated them with physically cross-linked polyvinyl alcohol-polyethylene glycol hydrogel (PVA-PEG), which served as an slow-release lubricant, showing excellent lubricating effect. Liang et al. [25] investigated the competitive coordination between CDs and polyols in lubrication system, successfully achieving liquid super-lubricity. However, the microscopic agglomeration during long-term storage remains a major challenge for practical application of CDs [26]. Surface modification of CDs is an effect approach to mitigate this issue and enhance their performance.

Ionic liquids (ILs), a novel class of synthetic lubricants, have garnered significant attention in the field of tribology [27]. ILs are versatile in their synthesis, allowing for the adaptation of molecular structure to meet specific working environment needs [28,29]. Cai et al. [30] conducted a comprehensive investigation into the friction mechanism of ionic liquids, indicating that strong polar ion pairs exhibit superior extrusion resistance compared to nonpolar oil molecules, which allows the lubrication film to adhere effectively onto the friction pairs surface at high shear force. Zhang et al. [31] fabricated poly(ionic liquid) functionalized COFs by grafting an initiator onto COFs surface and then surface-initiated polymerization of ionic liquid monomer. As a lubricant additive, this resulted in a significant reduction in an average COF to 0.094 and an impressive 82.9 % reduction in wear volume.

The self-assembly of catechols and their derivatives on various substrates through strong chemical bonds has been extensively studied [32–35]. In this study, we successfully synthesized a long-chain ionic liquid with catecholic group and was grafted to carbon dots surface (CDs-ILs-X) through catecholic molecular self-assembly, resulting in excellent dispersion stability in base oil. The tribological behaviors of CDs-ILs-Br as lubricant additive was evaluated, and at an addition concentration of 3.0 wt%, the average COF was decreased to 0.113, with a wear volume reduction of 60 %. Moreover, the introduction of antiwear agent BF4 - via anion exchange further decreased the average COF to 0.093. XPS analysis of the worn surface revealed a complex tribochemical reaction between CDs-ILs-BF4 and the friction pair matrix. The polar ionic liquid can adsorb onto the of carbon dots surface for enhancing the adhesion effect under high shear condition, facilitating the formation of a stable tribochemical protective film. This protective film played a crucial role in reducing friction and wear, as well as protecting the friction pairs.
All organic and inorganic chemicals were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd and Energy Chemical.

1-dodecyl-1H-imidazole
(2): KOH (10.9 g, 193.9 mmol) was added to a solution of imidazole (6 g, 88.1 mmol) in N,N-dimethylformamide (150 mL). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h, and then 1-bromododecane (1.1 equiv.) was added into the system and reflux at 85 ℃ for 6 h. After the reaction was complete, the mixture was diluted with water to 500 mL and extracted with CH2Cl2 (150 mL × 3), and the solvent was evaporated to yield a brown oily product. This product was purified using column chromatography to acquire a colorless liquid.

3-(2-carboxyethyl)−1-dodecyl-1H-imidazol-3-ium bromide
(3): A mixture of compound 2 (14.2 g, 60 mmol) and 3-bromopropanoic acid (1.1 equiv.) was stirred at 70 ℃ under N2 atmosphere for 6 h. After cooling, added 500 mL hexane and stir for 24 h, filter to obtain precipitation, and vacuum dried to obtain a white solid.[29].

3-(3-((3,4-dihydroxyphenethyl)amino)−3-oxopropyl)−1-dodecyl-1H-imidazol-3-ium bromide
(4) (Dop-ILs-Br): The mixture of compound 3 (11.4 g, 30 mmol), dopamine hydrochloride (1.0 equiv.), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (5 % mmol), N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (1.1 equiv.), and pyridine (15 mL) was stirred at ambient temperature for 18 h. Afterwards, the mixture was acidized with 2 M HCl to pH= 2, and the precipitation is removed by filtration. The resulting solution was extracted with CH2Cl2, and the solvent was evaporated to yield yellow crude product. This product was purified by silica gel chromatography to obtain a colorless liquid.

3-(3-((3,4-dihydroxyphenethyl)amino)−3-oxopropyl)−1-dodecyl-1H-imidazol-3-ium tetrafluoroborate
(6) (Dop-ILs-BF
4
): To a solution of compound 3 (10 mmol) in anhydrous acetonitrile (50 mL), ammonium tetrafluoroborate (2.61 g, 25 mmol) was added. The mixture was heated under nitrogen atmosphere at 70 ℃ for 24 h. After the reaction, the sediment is filtered out. The solvent was evaporated to obtain the product (compound 5). The mixture of compound 5, dopamine hydrochloride (1.0 equiv.), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (5 % mmol), N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (1.1 equiv.), and pyridine (15 mL) was stirred at ambient temperature for 28 h. The aftertreatment was similar to compound 4 to obtain a colorless liquid. (Scheme. S1).

The specific experimental procedure is as follows: Initially, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was dissolved at a concentration of 5 mg/mL in a 15 mL solution of 0.2 M NaOH. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined container (25 mL), and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. The product was collected by filtration using a 220 nm pore size membrane. The filtrate was then dialyzed with deionized water for 48 h to remove impurities (MWCO: 3500). Finally, the target compound was obtained as a powder through lyophilization.[36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ionic liquid-functionalized carbon dots. (CDs-ILs)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-ILs-Br: To a solution of CDs (200 mg) and Dop-ILs-Br (100 mg) in H2O: EtOH= 1: 1 mixed solution (40 mL), the mixture was ultrasonically treated for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixture was continuously stirred at ambient temperature for 36 h. After the reaction was complete, the resulting suspension was filtered, and the filter cake was then subjected to lyophilization to yield the pure powder.

CDs-ILs- BF
4 : The synthesis method is similar to CDs-ILs-Br.

The synthesis route of the Dop-ILs-Br is depicted in Scheme 1. Initially, an ionic liquid with catechol structure is synthesized through an amidation reaction. Subsequently, the resulting ionic liquid can self-assemble on the carbon dots surface through the anchoring effect of catecholic compound. The TEM image in Fig. 1e reveals the size and morphology of the CDs, showing a transverse size of approximately 2.8 ± 0.8 nm, which increases slightly to 4.3 ± 0.7 nm after modification with the ionic liquid. HR-TEM allows for the clear observation of lattice fringes with 0.21 nm spacing, corresponds to the (011) plane of graphite, confirming the good crystallinity of the sample [36]. Detailed characterization confirmed the successful grafting of the ionic liquid, as shown in Fig. 1b-d. Thermogravimetric analysis showed a significant weight loss of CDs-ILs-Br between 238 °C and 362 °C compared to the CDs, which is attributed to the decomposition of surface-grafted organic matter at high temperature [34,37]. XPS analysis showed that the N 1 s spectrum of the CDs showed a diffuse signal near 399.5 eV, which is attributed to the residual amino groups after the reduction of precursor. After the grafting of ionic liquid, a significantly stronger signal appeared at 401.5 eV, indicating the presence of a quaternary ammonium structure from the imidazole ring [31], thus confirming successful grafting of ionic liquid.

Furthermore, the surface potential of the carbon dots after modification also changes significantly. Initially, the potential of the carbon dots was 45 mV, which decreased to 16 mV after modification with ionic liquid. This change is due to the consumption of some active site, and the neutralization of part of the surface potential by the positively charged cationic group (Fig. S1).[38] The standardized UV–vis absorption analysis showed that a peak at 334 nm, due to the n→π * transition of CO bond, with enhanced absorption in the long-wave region due to the graphitization of the carbon core. The absorption peak at 280 nm is due to the absorption of CDs-ILs-Br (Fig. S2). The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d) of CDs-ILs-Br displayed a fingerprint region consistent with that of the CDs-ILs-Br in the 1600–1200 range, confirming the successful preparation of CDs-ILs-Br.

Dispersion stability is an important index for evaluating the compatibility of lubricant additive in base oil. We measured the dispersion size of CDs-ILs-Br in base oil by dynamic light scattering (DLS) over a 30 days period. As shown in Fig. 2, after the initial sample was treated with ultrasound, the dispersion of sample was uniform, with particle sizes ranging between 3–5 nm. As time prolongs, the approximate monodisperse diameter gradually increases at a slow rate, stabilizing after two weeks and eventually forming a poly-dispersed phase with particle sizes ranging from 2–20 nm. The oleophilic end of ionic liquid swells in the base oil, resulting in the moving particle size of carbon dots slightly larger than their actual size. In contrast, the unmodified carbon dots precipitated after one day of standing, and a large number of aggregates (300 nm) appeared in the base oil after three days (Fig. S3). This contrast reveals that the lipophilic ionic liquid significantly enhances the dispersion stability of the carbon dots.

As shown in Fig. 3a, the tribological behaviors of CDs and CDs-ILs-Br as additives in PEG200 were studied. Obvious lubrication failure is found in the COF curve of PEG200, characterized by a sharp rise in COF and fluctuation over time. This phenomenon was accompanied by the fluctuation of stroke, indicating serious adhesion between friction pairs. With the addition of 1.0 wt% CDs, there was a brief run-in phenomenon during which the COF rose sharply between 0–100 s before falling [39]. However, the COF could not remain stable after the run-in period, representing the same violent fluctuation as PEG200. The repetition of run-in period indicated that the addition of CDs only plays a slight anti-friction role, insufficient to protect the friction coiple from direct contact. In contrast, the COF curve of CDs-ILs-Br showed a short run-in phenomenon in the early stage of friction, their COF reaches its peak value at 100 s and then gradually decreases and stabilizes at about 0.1 after 10 min, entering the boundary lubrication stage. This suggests that an effective boundary lubricating film gradually formed on the friction surface, implying the important role of grafted ionic liquid in anti-friction. When the concentration of CDs-ILs-Br was increased to 3.0 wt%, the mean COF was lowest, and the curve was smoothest, identifying 3.0 wt% as the optimal concentration (Fig. S4). At the concentration higher than 3.0 wt%, excess CDs-ILs-Br accumulated at the friction interface, which was unfavorable for reducing COF and caused unnecessary losses.

In addition, the worn surfaces were analyzed using the three-dimensional surface profiler and SEM images. The wear volume of PEG200 is as high as 8.42 × 106 μm3, while that of CDs-ILs-Br is significantly lower at 3.5 × 106 μm3, a reduction of 60 % (Fig. 3b), which aligns well with its good anti-friction performance. The cross-section curves of the wear scars are exhibited in the Fig. 3c, there are a large number of furrows on the PEG200 lubricated surface, among which the largest furrow depth is up to 10 µm. This effect is significantly improved after the addition of CDs, but there are still obvious adhesive wear and abrasive wear on the worn surface, along with some shallow furrows (Fig. 3d). The rough worn surface of CDs is not conducive to the continuous adsorption of oil film, and the adhesion of local area on the friction pairs may cause the recurrence of run-in period. With the lubrication of CDs-ILs-Br, the surface roughness decreases significantly by 83 % (Ra=0.167 µm), with almost no adhesive wear observed. To further enhance the antifriction and anti-wear properties of CDs, the CDs-ILs-BF4 was synthesized via anion exchange method. The anion exchange rate was determined by analysis of composition and elemental content of the samples before and after anion exchange using XPS. As shown in Fig. 4, the signal at 67.5 eV in the Br 3d fine spectrum disappears and is replaced by the signal at 690.0 eV attributable to BF4 -, indicates that the anion exchange rate reaches nearly 99 %. At the concentration of 3.0 wt%, the tribological properties of CDs-ILs-BF4 were assessed and compared with those of CDs-ILs-Br. The average COF decreased to 0.093, and the wear volume was further reduced by 65 % (Fig. S5). The improvement in lubrication effect was particularly noticeable in the incipient stage of friction. The element distribution and morphology of worn surface based CDs-ILs-BF4 were examined using SEM and EDS. As displayed in Fig. 5b, there is no sign of adhesive or abrasive wear detected on the surface. Additionally, an enrichment of C element was observed on some shallow scratches, whereas the electronegative elements F and O were concentrated in deeper wear pit. This indicates that the CDs-ILs-BF4 effectively fills and repairs the friction pairs, enhancing lubrication and decreasing wear.

To elucidate the lubrication mechanism of CDs-ILs-BF4, XPS analysis was performed on wear scar surface. The C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 6a displays characteristic peaks at 284.8, 285.6, and 288.9 eV, assigned to C-C, C-O/C-N, and O-CO bonds, respectively [40]. These spectral features match those of the CDs-ILs-BF4 sample (Fig. S6), indicating that the CDs additive forms an adsorbed protective film on the frictional surface with enhanced shear resistance and mechanical stability. The signal at 283.7 eV in the lower binding energy region suggests metal carbide formation [41], illustrating that a portion of CDs-ILs-BF4 undergoes tribochemical reaction with the metal substrate, resulting in a robust tribochemical reaction film. The B 1 s and F 1 s signals provide compelling evidence for the active involvement of anti-wear ions in the formation of the protective film (Fig. 6c and S7). Their relatively weak signal intensity might be attributed to the preferential adsorption of the negatively charged BF4 - groups on the positively charged frictional surface, which are subsequently covered by carbon film. This observation is supported by the signal intensity observed in EDS (Fig. 5b). Additionally, the chemical state of Cu was analyzed (Fig. 6d), revealing clear indications of oxidation, predominantly characterized by CuO/Cu2O, which aligns with the O 1 s spectrum signal at of 530.2 eV (Fig. 6b). This indicates the formation of a robust metal oxide film on friction surface. The inherent lubricating properties and high mechanical strength of these metal oxides are crucial for augmenting the shear resistance of the friction film, thereby enhancing the overall tribological performance.

According to the above tribological test and characterization results, the proposed lubrication mechanism for CDs-ILs-BF4 is delineated in Scheme 2. Initially, the surface modification with ionic liquid imparts exceptional dispersion stability to the CDs in PEG200. This effective modification ensures that the CDs maintain their nanoscale dimension at microscopic level over an extended period, facilitating their penetration into the frictional interfaces and promptly filling in wear pits and micro-cracks. During the friction process, electrons emitted from the matrix of friction pairs cause the surface to become positively charged, attracting anions via electrostatic attraction. Consequently, the imidazolium cation of ionic liquid interacts with anions electrostatically, forming an alternating anion and cation adsorption film on the friction pairs. This adsorption process smoothens the surface, effectively polishing the frictional interfaces and thereby reducing the COF [31,42]. As frictional heat and mechanical forces accumulate, portions of the adsorbed film act as active sites for tribochemical reaction. Antiwear elements B and F from CDs-ILs-BF4 participate in the tribochemical reaction, contributing to the formation of a robust tribochemical protective film that connects with the metallic substrate. This film prevents the direct contact between friction pairs, thereby reducing wear volume. Overall, the synergistic effect of filling and polishing effects, and the tribochemical protective film, significantly diminish friction and wear, thereby enhancing the lubricating efficacy of base oil.

In general, we synthesized a novel long-chain ionic liquid incorporating a catechol structure and then self-assembled it on carbon dots surface to obtain ionic liquid functionalized carbon dots (CD-ILs-Br). The resulting CD-ILs-Br displayed exceptional dispersion stability for over one month and exhibited remarkable friction and wear resistance when tested as additive in PEG200. The introduction of the anti-wear ion BF4 - through anion exchange process further enhanced the lubricating properties, resulting in a significant reduction of the average COF to 0.093, with 65 % reduction in wear volume. These superior tribological properties are attributed to the enhanced adsorption effect between the grafted ionic liquid on carbon dots surface and the friction pairs, coupled with the improved dispersion capability that ensures adherence to the surface under high shear condition. Concurrently, a robust tribochemical protective film is formed as a result of tribochemical reactions, further contributing to the overall performance.
The authors declare that this manuscript is an original work, and the manuscript or part of it has not been published or submitted elsewhere.

Guangkai Jin: Data curation. Shujuan Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xiaozhi Zhang: Formal analysis. Yi Wang: Data curation. Qian Ye: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Weimin Liu: Writing – review & editing. Shenghua Xue: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A20623, U21A2046), the Western Light Project of CAS (xbzg-zdsys-202118), and Shaanxi Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Team (2024RS-CXTD-63).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.triboint.2024.110227.

ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
